6306e3725252479dd12943a6.question: Over footage of a film by this director, the narrator of a documentary describes how the world that centered on his school and his home on Elizabeth Street “got much bigger” watching films on TV. That narrator, an American director who briefly was part of this director’s family, added the word “my” to the title of one of their films for a four-hour documentary about the cinema of his country. That documentary also opens with one of this director’s scenes of an unconscious man floating down a (*) river on a small raft, with a sign labelling him a partisan. After some fascist-era films, Fellini’s first critically serious film credits are screenplays for this director’s “post-war trilogy”, though he would dramatically depart from this director’ style later. This director’s daughter, who starred in Death Becomes Her and Blue Velvet, was born to the Swedish star of several of his films, like Stromboli. For 10 points, identify this director of Germany, Year Zero and the father of Isabella, whose marriage to Ingrid Bergman coincided with the release of Voyage to Italy.

The Australian style of this genre, exemplified by films like Captain Fury and Raw Deal, is amusingly called “meat pie”. Duck, You Sucker and a trilogy by Sergio Sollima are examples of films in a variety of this genre named for Emiliano Zapata, which were more political than the musical style popular in Mexico during the 1930s. Pauline Kael described Alejandro Jodorowsky's El Topo as an (*) “acid” variety of this genre. Politically motivated American filmmakers in the 1960s began to challenge its traditional elements surrounding racial and gendered stereotypes in the “revisionist” variety of this genre made by directors like Richard Harris and Sam Peckinpah, who depicted incredible amounts of violence in the Wild Bunch. For 10 points, identify this genre of film popular in the 30s, 40, and 50s that Italian filmmakers like Sergio Leone made the “spaghetti” kind.

6306e3725252479dd12943ad.question: A book titled The Unspeakable Betrayal compiles writings of this director, including a 1959 essay in which he wrote about the lack of a necessary amount of mystery in film. This director began one film with a montage that tilts up on the Eiffel Tower while a voice-over describes problems that plague “almost every capital in the world”. A film by this director, which caused a riot that saw far-right Frenchman destroy a group of paintings, features a shot of several scalps on a cross, and begins with close-ups on (*) scorpions paired with intertitles describing their life. In a film by this director, the walls fall from the set as dining characters realize they are being observed by a live audience. A scene created by this director, often interpreted as an allegory for the weight of cultural mores, sees a character drag two men and pianos behind himself before a close-up on a hand ridden with ants. For 10 points, identify this Spanish director, a pioneering surrealist who collaborated with Salvador Dali on an iconic short film that features the slicing of an eyeball.

One thinker espoused this movement because of his belief that God was embodied in every frame of a film. Truffaut’s essay “A Certain Tendency of the French Cinema” is a vicious attack on one period of this movement. A style within this movement, which focused on working class characters and used long shots and takes, was its (*) “poetic” variety employed by Julien Duvivier and Marcel Carne and was succeeded by a “psychological” style. In a film from a different style of this movement, the camera shows the short protagonist through a large hole in the wall as he climbs up on furniture while carrying a suitcase. In another film in this style, the camera shows the central couple and a long line behind them through a small window of a pawn-shop, where they trade bed sheets for the title object needed for a job gluing posters. For 10 points, identify this general movement espoused by Andre Bazin and Bicycle Thieves director Vittorio De Sica, who was a leader in its Italian “neo-” variety.

6306e3725252479dd129441f.answer_sanitized: Romania [The Virgil clue is from The Death of Mr. Lazarescu]

6306e3725252479dd1294426.question: In a film by this director, a shot of the shadow of a man digging is followed by another shot of a man sitting still with a rifle propped up, only for his shadow to enter from the right and sit down as well. This director’s documentary about witch hunts, made during Nazi occupation, defined his late career’s style of very long shots at a distance that strongly contrasted with his silent era style. In one scene, this director’s camera spends almost three minutes slowly rotating around a girl standing behind her seated uncle as they talk about her (*) dying mother. For perhaps this director’s most famous film, he cast the madman playwright Antoine Artaud as a monk sympathetic to the title character. In that film, this director used low angles and harsh lighting for characters interrogating the title character played by Renée Jeanne Falconetti, who is shown crying in iconic close-ups with soft lighting as she explains the message she received from God. For 10 points, identify this Danish director of Gertrude and Ordet, who made the enormously influential silent film The Passion of Joan of Arc.

6306e3725252479dd1294429.question: In one film, this director’s camera shakes as it follows a man turn the corner of an outdoor hallway, where a woman in another scene gets decked while running by a man opening his front door. In the last shot of one of this director’s films, a detective standing in a country road looks into the camera after a young girl tells him about an ordinary man returning to a crime scene; the actor in that shot, (*) Song-Kang Ho, has been in four of this director’s films. Two films by this director with similar environmental themes include about the conflict over a massive, experimental pig and the other about the residents of the Tail Section of a train that never stops. At the start of one of this director’s most recent film, a poor high school student lies about being in university to begin a tutor job for the wealth Park family. For 10 points, identify this Korean director behind dark and peculiar films like the monster movie the Netflix film Okja, the post-apocalyptic Snowpiercer, and the 2019 Palme D’or winner Parasite.
